acara_description: Create dot plots and stem-and-leaf plots; describe and compare distributions by referring to shape, centre and spread.

questions.stem: In a stem-and-leaf plot, the stem '3' with leaves '2, 4, 8' represents which values?

default: Stem-and-leaf: the stem is the tens digit, the leaf is the units digit.

questions.stem: A dot plot shows dots above the values 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6. What is the mode?

default: The mode is the value with the most dots.
5 has three dots — more than any other.
Mode = 5.

questions.stem: A stem-and-leaf plot shows: 2|3,5,7 and 3|1,4. How many data values are there?

default: Count all the leaves.
Stem 2: leaves 3, 5, 7 → 3 values. Stem 3: leaves 1, 4 → 2 values.

questions.worked_solution: 3 leaves for stem 2 + 2 leaves for stem 3 = 5 values.

questions.stem: From the dot plot with values (one dot each): 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 7, find the median.

default: There are 8 values. Order them.
Ordered: 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 7. With 8 values, median = average of 4th and 5th.

questions.stem: What is a stem-and-leaf plot best used for?
options: Displaying small to medium numerical datasets

answer.correct: Displaying small to medium numerical datasets

default: Stem-and-leaf plots organise numerical data while preserving individual values.
They work well when there are roughly 10–50 data points.
They're not suitable for very large datasets.

questions.worked_solution: Stem-and-leaf plots are best for displaying and analysing small to medium numerical datasets.

questions.stem: In a dot plot, what does each dot represent?

default: Every individual data point gets one dot placed above its value.
So 3 dots above 5 means the value 5 occurs 3 times.
Each dot = one data value.

questions.worked_solution: In a dot plot, each dot represents one individual data value.

questions.stem: From stem-and-leaf: 2|4,6 and 3|0,2,8. Find the mean.
A dataset is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100}. Which statement is true about the mean and median?
options: Mean is much higher than median
Median is much higher than mean

answer.correct: Mean is much higher than median

default: The outlier 100 pulls the mean upward.
Mean ≈ 19.2, median = 3.5. Mean is much higher.

questions.worked_solution: Outlier pulls mean high: mean ≈ 19.2. Median = 3.5. Mean >> median.

questions.stem: A back-to-back stem-and-leaf plot is useful for:
options: Comparing two datasets side by side

answer.correct: Comparing two datasets side by side

default: Back-to-back means two sets of leaves on either side of the stems.
This allows visual comparison of two groups.
For example, compare heights of boys and girls.

questions.worked_solution: Back-to-back stem-and-leaf plots compare two datasets sharing the same stems.

questions.stem: A dot plot is right-skewed (tail to the right). This means:
options: Most data is on the right
Most data is clustered on the left with a few high values on the right

answer.correct: Most data is clustered on the left with a few high values on the right

default: Right-skewed (positive skew) means the tail extends to the right.
Most data clusters at lower values; a few high outliers create the tail.
The mean is pulled right by the outliers, so mean > median.

questions.worked_solution: Right skew: cluster of data on left, long tail extending to higher values on right.
